name: Standardization Advocacy Strategy
consequences: Adopting open standards immediately increases compatibility but systemically reduces vendor lock-in and strategically shifts power dynamics toward interoperability. 25% faster scaling through cross-platform adoption, but risks delayed implementation due to industry inertia.
options: Incremental adoption of FIDO2/WebAuthn with limited platform-specific adaptations
Collaborative development of a Danish eIDAS2 compatibility layer for non-Google/Apple platforms
Blockchain-based decentralized identity verification as a radical alternative to centralized trust models
review: Controls Interoperability vs. Industry Adoption. Weakness: The options fail to consider the regulatory lag in approving decentralized identity frameworks.

name: Procurement Influence Strategy
consequences: Mandating platform-neutral requirements immediately raises vendor compliance costs but systemically diversifies the supplier base and strategically forces institutional reevaluation of dependency risks. 30% reduction in single-source procurement, but weakens short-term vendor relationships.
options: Lobbying for explicit platform-neutrality clauses in AltID procurement contracts
Creating a public-private certification program for non-platform-dependent identity solutions
Implementing a dynamic scoring system that penalizes supplier concentration in tender evaluations
review: Controls Short-Term Compliance vs. Long-Term Innovation. Weakness: The options understate the risk of regulatory capture by existing platform vendors.

name: Coalition Building Strategy
consequences: Forming cross-sector alliances immediately amplifies advocacy reach but systemically creates coordination overhead and strategically risks dilution of core objectives. 40% higher stakeholder engagement, but increases vulnerability to conflicting interests.
options: Establishing a formal consortium with civil society, academia, and privacy advocates
Creating a neutral technical advisory board with platform vendor representatives
Launching a public awareness campaign framing digital sovereignty as a civic duty
review: Controls Advocacy Reach vs. Organizational Cohesion. Weakness: The options neglect the potential for internal factionalism among coalition members.

name: Demonstrator Development Strategy
consequences: Building technical proofs of concept immediately validates feasibility but systemically diverts resources from policy work and strategically creates leverage in procurement negotiations. 50% higher technical credibility, but risks being dismissed as non-production-ready.
options: Developing a minimal viable demonstrator for GrapheneOS-based authentication
Creating a browser-based fallback solution using open-source identity frameworks
Implementing a hardware token prototype with quantum-resistant cryptography
review: Controls Technical Credibility vs. Resource Allocation. Weakness: The options don't address the challenge of maintaining demonstrator relevance amid rapidly changing platform security standards.

name: Regulatory Framing Strategy
consequences: Reframing the issue as a resilience imperative immediately shifts policy priorities but systemically challenges existing regulatory frameworks and strategically creates precedent for European-wide standards. 20% faster regulatory adoption, but increases legal uncertainty.
options: Positioning platform neutrality as a critical infrastructure requirement
Advocating for mandatory contingency planning in digital identity procurement
Proposing a European-wide certification standard for fallback authentication mechanisms
review: Controls Policy Momentum vs. Legal Complexity. Weakness: The options understate the challenge of aligning national regulations with EU-wide frameworks.

name: Alternative Authentication Ecosystem Strategy
consequences: Developing alternative authentication methods immediately diversifies access points but systemically challenges existing platform dominance and strategically creates new market dynamics. 30% faster adoption in niche sectors, but risks fragmentation without standardization.
options: Gradual integration of biometric authentication with existing platforms
Collaborative development of a national open-source authentication framework
Decentralized identity verification using blockchain-based self-sovereign identity models
review: Controls Fragmentation vs. Standardization. Weakness: The options understate the risk of interoperability failures between alternative systems.

name: Open-Source Ecosystem Development
consequences: Prioritizing open-source solutions immediately lowers vendor dependency but systemically accelerates community-driven innovation and strategically shifts development control to decentralized actors. 20% cost reduction in prototype phases, but weakens proprietary support channels.
options: Leveraging existing open-source identity frameworks with minimal customization
Establishing a Danish open-source identity consortium with EU partners
Building a modular identity stack using quantum-resistant cryptographic libraries
review: Controls Cost Efficiency vs. Proprietary Support. Weakness: The options neglect the challenge of sustaining long-term open-source maintenance without commercial backing.

name: Cross-Border Digital Resilience Strategy
consequences: Aligning with Nordic and EU digital resilience initiatives immediately strengthens collective bargaining power but systemically creates regulatory complexity and strategically diversifies geopolitical risk exposure. 15% higher compliance costs, but reduces single-market vulnerability.
options: Forming a Nordic identity resilience coalition for shared technical standards
Adopting EU-wide eIDAS2 compatibility as a baseline for all demonstrators
Creating a pan-European fallback authentication network with mutual recognition
review: Controls Geopolitical Risk vs. Regulatory Complexity. Weakness: The options fail to address potential conflicts between national and EU regulatory priorities.

name: Legal Innovation Framework Strategy
consequences: Proposing novel legal constructs immediately redefines accountability boundaries but systemically creates precedents for future digital governance and strategically challenges existing liability frameworks. 25% faster policy iteration, but increases litigation exposure.
options: Introducing liability-sharing agreements between identity providers and platform vendors
Drafting a Danish Digital Sovereignty Act with mandatory fallback requirements
Implementing dynamic legal clauses that adapt to platform policy changes
review: Controls Legal Precedent vs. Litigation Risk. Weakness: The options overlook the challenge of enforcing novel legal frameworks against powerful platform incumbents.

name: Public-Private Synergy Strategy
consequences: Fostering collaboration between state and private actors immediately builds practical implementation pathways but systemically creates power imbalances and strategically risks co-optation by incumbent vendors. 40% higher stakeholder buy-in, but weakens independent oversight.
options: Establishing a public-private identity innovation lab with shared governance
Creating a vendor-neutral certification program for identity solutions
Deploying a pilot program with select private-sector partners for real-world testing
review: Controls Stakeholder Buy-In vs. Independent Oversight. Weakness: The options don't address the risk of private-sector influence undermining public-interest objectives.

name: Trust Infrastructure Reconfiguration Strategy
consequences: Redesigning trust models immediately destabilizes platform-centric authentication but systemically enables decentralized verification and strategically shifts accountability to institutional actors. 20% faster trust validation through distributed architectures, but risks operational complexity in transition.
options: Incremental shift to institutional-issued digital credentials with limited platform integration
Collaborative development of a national trust registry for cross-platform verification
Decentralized identity verification using zero-knowledge proofs as a radical alternative
review: Controls Centralized Control vs. Decentralized Autonomy. Weakness: The options understate the challenge of institutional capacity to manage distributed trust systems.

name: Interoperability Assurance Strategy
consequences: Implementing interoperability protocols immediately reduces technical barriers but systemically forces platform vendors to adapt and strategically creates a baseline for cross-system compatibility. 35% faster integration between legacy and new systems, but increases short-term development costs.
options: Adopting a phased interoperability framework with platform-specific adapters
Creating a public interoperability certification body for identity solutions
Developing a universal identity translation layer using open APIs
review: Controls Technical Compatibility vs. Vendor Resistance. Weakness: The options fail to account for platform vendors' potential to delay or obstruct interoperability mandates.

name: Vendor Agnosticism Framework Strategy
consequences: Designing vendor-agnostic systems immediately limits platform-specific dependencies but systemically diversifies procurement options and strategically reduces long-term vendor lock-in. 25% lower procurement risk through diversified supplier base, but weakens immediate technical support channels.
options: Building modular identity systems with interchangeable authentication modules
Establishing a vendor-agnostic certification standard for identity solutions
Implementing a dynamic vendor evaluation system based on compliance metrics
review: Controls Short-Term Flexibility vs. Long-Term Stability. Weakness: The options neglect the risk of fragmented vendor ecosystems creating new interoperability challenges.

name: Digital Resilience Governance Strategy
consequences: Establishing governance structures for resilience immediately formalizes contingency planning but systemically institutionalizes risk management and strategically creates accountability for service continuity. 15% higher compliance with resilience standards, but increases bureaucratic overhead.
options: Creating a national digital resilience oversight committee with cross-sector representation
Implementing mandatory resilience audit requirements for identity providers
Developing a real-time resilience monitoring dashboard for critical systems
review: Controls Institutional Accountability vs. Bureaucratic Burden. Weakness: The options understate the challenge of aligning governance structures with rapidly evolving technical threats.

name: Authentication Ecosystem Diversification Strategy
consequences: Expanding authentication methods immediately reduces platform reliance but systemically creates multiple access pathways and strategically enhances user choice and system robustness. 40% broader access options for citizens, but increases complexity in user education and support.
options: Introducing multi-modal authentication with biometric, hardware, and software options
Creating a federated authentication network across public and private sectors
Deploying a quantum-resistant authentication layer as a radical future-proofing measure
review: Controls User Accessibility vs. System Complexity. Weakness: The options don't address the potential for user confusion in managing multiple authentication methods.
